text.title: Sprint Goal Formula Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that produce a single-sentence sprint goal the team can recite and the review can grade pass/fail.

rule.statement: Every sprint MUST have exactly one written sprint goal containing all three formula parts: (a) outcome — verb + user/system change, (b) boundary — explicit in/out scope edge, (c) measure — verifiable signal. A goal missing any part fails the planning gate.
rule.rationale: One-or-two-part goals collapse under pressure: a goal with no measure cannot be graded; a goal with no boundary invites scope creep; a goal with no outcome is a task list. All three parts together make the goal operational.

rule.statement: The sprint goal MUST fit in one sentence under 30 words. If it cannot, the sprint is targeting two goals and MUST be either split or de-scoped.
rule.rationale: Compactness is the recitability test. A goal the team cannot remember at standup does not function as a goal — it functions as documentation.

rule.statement: Any scope addition raised mid-sprint MUST be graded against the goal using the binary test: "does this advance the outcome inside the boundary?" If yes → may enter the sprint with PM approval. If no → goes to next sprint backlog. A "maybe / sort of" answer counts as no.
rule.rationale: The "maybe" path is how sprint goals die: each individually small addition seems aligned enough, and the goal silently drifts. The binary test forces a clean ruling.

rule.statement: At sprint review, the measure portion of the goal MUST be evaluated as pass or fail — no partial credit, no narrative qualifier. A failed measure triggers a retrospective root-cause discussion as the first agenda item.
rule.rationale: Soft grading erodes the goal's signal: if "kind of passed" is allowed, the formula stops shaping behaviour. Binary grading + automatic retro link makes failure productive instead of demoralising.

rule.statement: A team MUST NOT commit to two concurrent sprint goals. If two genuinely independent outcomes are needed, the team is over-allocated; the PM MUST escalate to staffing or descope one outcome before sprint start.
rule.rationale: "Two goals" is the second-place coping pattern after "no goal" — it preserves the appearance of focus while removing the function. Enforcing single-goal-per-sprint forces the real conversation about capacity.

example.code:
Goal: Reduce new-user signup drop-off by enabling email-only signup for web (not mobile), measured by signup completion rate ≥ 65% on staging cohort.
Parts: outcome = reduce new-user signup drop-off via email-only flow boundary = web only, not mobile measure = signup completion rate ≥ 65% on staging cohort
example.note: One sentence under 30 words. Outcome, boundary, and measure all present and falsifiable.

example.code: Goal: "Improve the checkout experience and clean up tech debt."
example.note: Two goals, no boundary, no measure. At review, nothing can be graded. Fails the formula on every rule.
